title: The Science of Synesthesia During Psychedelic States
body:
The Science of Synesthesia During Psychedelic States

1. Opening Hook: When Colors Sing and Sounds Bloom

It was the middle of the night in a quiet room, soft pillows scattered around me as I sat with a small dose of psilocybin mushrooms. I closed my eyes and suddenly the music wasn’t just sound - it was a river of colors flowing across my eyelids, each note painting the space with vibrant hues. The bass wasn’t just heard; it was felt like ripples of shadowy blue washing through my body. At the same time, a soft scent drifted through the air, and I could almost taste its warmth on my tongue. I was awash in a sensory experience where the lines between seeing, hearing, tasting, and smelling blurred or straight-up disappeared. This isn’t just poetic whimsy or a one-off oddity. This doorway to mixed senses is a well-documented phenomenon called synesthesia, and it often shows up in psychedelic experiences.

Have you ever wondered why these cross-sensory experiences happen? Why does a certain sound evoke a color or a shape take on a taste? As someone who’s spent years exploring the therapeutic and mind-expanding worlds of mushrooms and psychedelics, I’ve seen firsthand how synesthesia offers more than just a curious side effect - it can be a deep window into how our brains construct reality and how psychedelics temporarily rewire those processes. The idea that our senses are hard-wired to stay separate is more of a default setting than a rule carved in stone. Psychedelics throw a wrench in the default wiring, inviting sensory pathways to mingle.

Before I go any deeper, I’ll admit this feeling can be disorienting, even overwhelming at times. But when handled with respect and curiosity, synesthesia during psychedelic journeys can reveal new ways of perceiving and relating to the world. It’s like your brain is playing a new instrument, creating a symphony where you can see tastes or hear colors. For the uninitiated, it might sound like madness. For those who’ve experienced it, it’s a thrilling, often humbling realization that our senses are far more connected than we give them credit for.

So what’s really going on behind the scenes? How does the brain, under the influence of psychedelics like psilocybin or LSD, break down those sensory barriers? More importantly, how can understanding this phenomenon help us in therapy, creativity, or healing? I’m going to take you through what synesthesia is, the science supporting it, the research led by folks like Robin Carhart-Harris and Roland Griffiths, practical ways to engage with these experiences, what hurdles you might face, and how it can stick with you long after the trip ends. To get a full picture, I’m also recommending a few essential reads along the way, like How to Change Your Mind by Michael Pollan, which does a stellar job explaining how psychedelics disrupt and rebuild perception.

2. Core Concept Explanation: What is Synesthesia and How Psychedelics Trigger It

Synesthesia is a neurological phenomenon where stimulation of one sensory modality involuntarily triggers a perception in another. For example, someone might hear a sound and automatically see a color, or read a word and taste a flavor. This cross-activation occurs in about 4 percent of the general population under normal circumstances - people whose brains have slightly different wiring or connectivity patterns.

In psychedelic states, synesthesia occurs more broadly and temporarily. The substances like psilocybin, LSD, and mescaline all interact with serotonin receptors in the brain, particularly the 5-HT2A receptor. This receptor is abundant in the cortex, especially in regions responsible for integrating sensory information, such as the visual cortex and the association areas. When psychedelics bind to these receptors, they disrupt the brain's usual filtering and processing of sensory input.

Under normal conditions, the brain keeps sensory modalities relatively compartmentalized to avoid sensory confusion. The thalamus acts as a hub that filters sensory information before it reaches the cortex. Psychedelics appear to reduce the gating function of the thalamus, allowing more sensory information to flood into the brain’s higher centers. At the same time, psychedelics decrease activity in the default mode network, a set of brain regions linked to our sense of self and habitual thought patterns. This reduction may dissolve the boundaries between senses, allowing them to blend.

What’s fascinating is that psychedelics not only increase connectivity within sensory regions but also between areas that normally don’t interact much. This hyper-connected state means the auditory cortex might communicate more freely with the visual cortex, explaining why you might "see" sounds or "hear" colors. This cross-modal integration creates the vivid, often dreamlike sensory overlap that defines synesthesia during a trip.

Neuroscientist David Eagleman, who has studied synesthesia extensively, explains that we all have the neural substrates for these cross-sensory connections - but they’re usually suppressed. Psychedelics lift the veil. For people predisposed to synesthesia, the experience may be even more intense. For others, these mixed sensations are temporary, fading as the drug wears off and the brain rewires itself back to its usual patterns.

Interestingly, synesthesia isn't just some random effect. It’s linked to creativity, memory, and even empathy. Psychedelic-induced synesthesia could offer clues about how our brain’s sensory systems are more flexible than we realize, a concept that researchers like Roland Griffiths and Robin Carhart-Harris have touched on while studying the psychedelic brain. This melding of senses can produce a state of awe or wonder, which might be part of why so many people report lasting shifts in perspective after their experiences.

3. Research and Evidence: What Studies Tell Us About Psychedelic Synesthesia

The scientific study of synesthesia in psychedelic states has grown substantially in recent years. One of the landmark figures in this area is Roland Griffiths, whose work at Johns Hopkins University has shed light on how psilocybin changes perception. In a 2011 study published in Psychopharmacology, Griffiths and colleagues gave volunteers moderate to high doses of psilocybin and found that a significant number reported synesthetic experiences, like seeing colors when hearing music. These reports weren’t just anecdotal - they correlated with measurable changes in brain activity seen through functional MRI scans.

Robin Carhart-Harris, another leading scientist in psychedelic research, has taken this further by mapping how psychedelics alter brain networks. His 2016 paper in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences demonstrated that psychedelics increase global connectivity across the brain, including between sensory and association areas. This supports the idea that synesthesia arises because psychedelics scramble normal sensory boundaries. Carhart-Harris’s team observed an increase in what they called "entropy" in the brain - a state of higher unpredictability and communication across regions that normally stay more isolated.

There’s also research from neuroscientist David Eagleman, who is known for his studies on synesthesia outside psychedelic contexts. Eagleman argues that synesthesia might be an exaggeration of the brain’s natural cross-sensory connections and that psychedelics artificially amplify this effect. His experiments with sensory substitution devices show how flexible our sensory perceptions can be, lending support to the psychedelic synesthesia model.

Stanislav Grof, a psychiatrist who’s been working with LSD since the 1950s, documented countless subjective reports of synesthesia during psychedelic sessions. Although Grof’s work was more qualitative, it offered invaluable insight into how these experiences affect consciousness and healing. His theories influenced later researchers who sought to tie these sensory phenomena to emotional breakthroughs and trauma processing.

More recently, a study published in 2021 in Frontiers in Neuroscience measured synesthetic responses during controlled psychedelic sessions. The researchers found that participants’ reports of color-sound synesthesia correlated with changes in the connectivity of the auditory and visual cortex. The study suggested that synesthesia could be a biomarker for the degree of psychedelic-induced brain network rewiring.

If you want to explore the scientific and personal dimensions of how psychedelics alter perception and cognition, Michael Pollan’s How to Change Your Mind remains a must-read. Pollan’s narrative integrates the research with firsthand accounts of sensory blending, underscoring how these experiences might be linked to therapeutic outcomes.

4. Practical Application: Engaging with Synesthesia During Psychedelic Sessions

Experiencing synesthesia while on psychedelics can be thrilling, confusing, or even unsettling. But if you’re prepared, it can also become a valuable part of your journey. Here are some ways you can engage with and learn from those cross-sensory moments.

First, setting the right intention is key. Before your session, think about what you want from the experience. If you’re open to exploring sensory blending, try bringing along music with rich textures and a variety of instruments or sounds. Many people find that carefully selected playlists can trigger or enhance synesthetic experiences. I personally love ambient or psychedelic rock for this purpose - something immersive but not overwhelming.

Second, use the sensory mixing as a form of active curiosity. When you notice the music turning into colors or smells triggering shapes, try describing these sensations in a journal. Writing down what you’re experiencing in real time can deepen your awareness and create a record that you can later reflect on. A beautiful option is to use a Journaling Notebook Leather Bound, which encourages slow, thoughtful writing and adds a physical ritual element to your integration.

Third, let these moments gently guide your attention inward. Synesthesia can be a reminder that your brain’s filters are down, and your usual sensory boundaries don’t apply. Instead of resisting or trying to control the experience, surrender to it and see where it takes you emotionally and imaginatively. This can open doors to new insights, memories, or creative ideas.

Finally, if you’re working with a therapist or guide, share your sensory experiences openly. People like Bill Richards and Michael Mithoefer, pioneers in psychedelic-assisted therapy, emphasize the importance of talking through unusual sensations to ground and make sense of them. Synesthetic experiences can sometimes reveal hidden emotional material or shifts in perspective that are crucial for healing. For example, a client might realize that the colors they see during music symbolize unresolved grief or joy.

It’s worth noting that this sensory blending doesn’t just happen with mushrooms or LSD. Ayahuasca, mescaline, and even cannabis can produce milder forms of synesthesia, especially when combined with focused attention or meditation. For anyone curious about these states, practicing mindfulness during the session can increase the likelihood of noticing and engaging with cross-modal sensations.

5. Common Challenges and How to Work Through Them

Not every encounter with synesthesia during psychedelics is effortless or pleasant. For many, the flooding of sensory channels can be overwhelming, disorienting, or even frightening. You might hear loud noises or see chaotic colors that don’t feel harmonious, leading to anxiety or panic. This is especially common in folks new to psychedelics or those with a history of trauma or mental health challenges.

One of the biggest hurdles is losing a sense of grounding. When your senses mix wildly, it can be hard to keep your bearings in time and space. Common advice is to have familiar objects or textures nearby - a soft blanket, a comforting scent, or a steady voice to anchor you. This can help calm the nervous system when the sensory noise becomes too intense.

Another challenge is the unpredictability of synesthetic experiences. They can come and go without warning, making it hard to prepare mentally. Accepting that some loss of control is part of the psychedelic territory can ease resistance. If anxiety flares, deep breathing and focusing on your body can bring you back to a more stable place. Books like The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk offer invaluable tools for working with trauma and bodily sensations, which can be useful if you find sensory overlap triggering past wounds.

Sometimes synesthesia can blur boundaries so much that it complicates emotional processing. Feeling like your senses are colliding may make it hard to focus on one feeling or thought. In these cases, journaling after the experience - once the peak has passed - can help unpack what happened. Writing can translate the mixed sensory input into coherent reflections, allowing you to track emotional themes or patterns.

For people prone to migraines or sensory processing disorders, psychedelic synesthesia might exacerbate symptoms. Starting with very low doses and working with experienced guides can minimize risks. It’s also crucial to honor your own limits and not push into experiences that feel destabilizing. Preparing with grounding practices like yoga or meditation beforehand can build nervous system resilience.

Finally, if synesthesia leads to confusion about what’s “real,” it’s worth remembering that the brain is showing you its flexibility. These experiences are temporary and reversible. Learning to observe without judgment can transform fear into fascination. Over time, you can develop better tools to ride the waves of sensory mixing, turning what once felt like chaos into creative fuel.

6. Integration with Daily Life: Carrying the Echoes of Synesthesia Beyond the Trip

One of the most fascinating things about synesthesia during psychedelic experiences is how it can ripple outward long after the drug has left your system. Even if the colors and sounds don’t mingle every day, many people notice a lasting shift in how they perceive the world. This can show up as heightened sensory awareness, increased creativity, or a richer emotional life.

For artists, musicians, and writers, psychedelic synesthesia often unlocks new ways of expressing ideas. Seeing music as color, for example, can inspire a painter to blend hues in novel ways or a composer to experiment with tonal arrangements that “match” certain palettes. This kind of cross-sensory insight is why many creatives have turned to psychedelics for inspiration. The poet Anne Waldman spoke about how psychedelic experiences made her words take on new shapes and sounds, adding depth to her work.

Even outside the arts, synesthesia can deepen your connection to everyday experiences. You might find a song you liked before suddenly “glows” with more vivid emotion, or notice that certain tastes bring up mental images you never saw before. These subtle changes can make life feel more textured and alive. They’re reminders that our sensory experience is more fluid than we usually assume.

Integrating these experiences requires gentle attention and care. Keeping a journal, especially a dedicated one like the Journaling Notebook Leather Bound I mentioned earlier, is a powerful way to track shifts in perception and mood over weeks and months. Writing down moments when sensory associations arise helps them become part of your conscious story rather than fleeting curiosities.

Therapists trained in psychedelic integration, such as those following the approaches outlined by Fadiman or Mithoefer, encourage clients to explore the symbolic meaning of their synesthetic impressions. For example, a particular color that appears during music might relate to an emotion or memory. Working through these connections can promote healing and self-understanding.

Science also suggests that psychedelics can enhance neuroplasticity for days or even weeks post-trip. This means your brain is more open to forming new habits and perspectives. Synesthetic experiences invite you to practice perceiving the world with fresh eyes - or ears, or tongue. Mindfulness exercises that focus on sensory awareness can help maintain the state of openness initiated by the psychedelic journey.

In a way, psychedelic synesthesia is like a key to peek behind the curtain of your own perception. By bringing those experiences into your daily life, they can gently shift how you engage with art, people, nature, and even your inner world. That’s a rare gift, and it’s one I hope more people learn to explore thoughtfully.

7. Closing Reflection: What Synesthesia Teaches Us About Being Human

If I step back and reflect on what synesthesia during psychedelics really means, it feels like a deep invitation to reconsider the solidity of reality itself. We like to think the world shows up the same way for everyone - that each sense is exclusive, that colors belong with the eyes, sounds with the ears. But synesthesia cracks this tidy arrangement wide open, revealing perception as something more fluid, more interconnected.

What does it say about our brains that these sensory barriers can be so easily bent, shifted, and blended? It suggests that our experience of reality isn’t a fixed truth but a creative act our minds perform moment by moment. Psychedelics peel back the layers of conditioning, letting us glimpse the raw process of how senses fuse to create meaning.

There’s a poetic magic in synesthesia, but it’s not just about wonder or novelty. It’s a clue that the brain is a living structure, always in motion, capable of surprising change. It also nudges us toward empathy. If our senses can overlap so dramatically, maybe our perceptions of each other are just as malleable and rich, waiting to be expanded beyond assumptions.

Listening to colors or tasting sounds might sound like a party trick, but it’s also a reminder that boundaries - between senses, between people, between ideas - are often more permeable than we assume. The work of researchers like Grof and Griffiths helps ground this mystery in real science, while the personal stories of those who’ve journeyed with psychedelics bring it to life. I encourage anyone curious about these experiences to hold synesthesia lightly - not as a goal or a badge, but as a doorway to new ways of seeing what’s been in front of us all along.

And as you remember the next time music “shines” with a color or a moment tastes like sunlight, perhaps you’ll carry with you a deeper sense of how rich and strange our world really is. That is something worth sitting quietly with, long after the trip ends.
